era_context: The early 1730s belong to the mature Enlightenment and the intensifying competition among European great powers. France, Britain, Austria, Russia, Spain, and the Ottoman Empire were the dominant states, with the Qing dynasty ruling China, the Mughal Empire fragmenting in India, and Tokugawa Japan in self-imposed isolation. Colonial expansion in the Americas continued, while in Persia, the military strongman Nadir was consolidating power. Intellectual life was marked by Enlightenment philosophy, scientific advances, and the flourishing of Baroque and early Classical arts.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa beyond coastal trading posts and the major kingdoms (Ashanti, Dahomey)
Interior Southeast Asia (Cambodia, Laos, inland Burma)
Interior South America beyond Iberian colonial zones

events.title: Death of Augustus II and the Polish succession crisis
events.description: Augustus II ('the Strong'), Elector of Saxony and King of Poland, died on 1 February 1733 in Warsaw. His death triggered an immediate succession crisis in the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, as both French-backed Stanisław Leszczyński and Austro-Russian-backed Augustus III of Saxony claimed the throne. This set the stage for the War of the Polish Succession, one of the major European conflicts of the decade. Augustus II's long reign had tied Saxony to Poland and entangled the Commonwealth in the Great Northern War and ongoing great-power rivalries.
key_figures: Augustus II (King of Poland and Elector of Saxony)
Stanisław Leszczyński (rival claimant, father-in-law of Louis XV)

sources.name: Jerzy Lukowski, Liberty's Folly: The Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth in the Eighteenth Century (1991)
Saxon court records and contemporary diplomatic correspondence

events.certainty_note: Death date and political consequences are firmly established in numerous contemporary sources.
cross_references: 1738-xxx (Treaty of Vienna ending the War of the Polish Succession)

events.title: Election of Stanisław Leszczyński as King of Poland
events.description: On 12 September 1733, a faction of the Polish-Lithuanian szlachta (nobility) elected Stanisław Leszczyński as King of Poland at a convocation Sejm near Warsaw. Leszczyński, who had previously been king during the Great Northern War (1704–1709) with Swedish backing, was now supported by France, as he was father-in-law to Louis XV. However, a rival faction backed by Russia and Austria refused to recognize his election and convened a counter-election in October that chose Augustus III of Saxony, precipitating the War of the Polish Succession.
key_figures: Louis XV (King of France, his son-in-law)
Cardinal André-Hercule de Fleury (French chief minister)

sources.name: Jerzy Lukowski, Liberty's Folly: The Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth in the Eighteenth Century (1991)
French diplomatic correspondence, Archives du Ministère des Affaires Étrangères

events.certainty_note: Election date and circumstances documented in multiple contemporary diplomatic and Polish sources.

events.title: War of the Polish Succession begins
events.description: The contested Polish election escalated into a general European war in October 1733. France, Spain, and Sardinia allied against Austria and Russia. While the casus belli was the Polish throne, much of the actual fighting occurred in the Rhineland and Italy, where France and its allies sought to weaken Habsburg power. Russia sent troops into Poland to install Augustus III by force. The war ultimately reshaped territorial holdings in Italy and confirmed Russian dominance over Polish affairs.
key_figures: Cardinal André-Hercule de Fleury (French chief minister, directing war policy)

sources.name: John L. Sutton, The King's Honor and the King's Cardinal: The War of the Polish Succession (1980)
Contemporary diplomatic dispatches and declarations of war

events.certainty_note: Major European war with extensive documentary record across multiple states.

events.title: First Family Compact between France and Spain
events.description: On 7 November 1733, France and Spain signed the Treaty of the Escorial, known as the First Family Compact (Pacte de Famille). This alliance between the two Bourbon monarchies committed them to mutual support against the Habsburgs in the War of the Polish Succession. Spain's goal was to recover territories in Italy lost at Utrecht (1713), particularly Naples and Sicily for the Spanish Infante Don Carlos. The compact established a pattern of Franco-Spanish Bourbon cooperation that would recur throughout the 18th century in the Second (1743) and Third (1761) Family Compacts.
key_figures: Elisabeth Farnese (Queen consort of Spain, driving Italian ambitions)

sources.name: Treaty text preserved in diplomatic archives; Recueil des traités
Alfred Baudrillart, Philippe V et la Cour de France (1890–1901)

events.certainty_note: Treaty date and terms well documented in both French and Spanish archives.

events.title: Founding of the Colony of Georgia
events.description: On 12 February 1733, James Oglethorpe and approximately 120 colonists landed at Yamacraw Bluff and founded the settlement of Savannah, establishing the colony of Georgia — the last of the thirteen British colonies that would later form the United States. Georgia was chartered in 1732 as a philanthropic venture to resettle debtors and 'worthy poor' from England, and as a strategic buffer between British Carolina and Spanish Florida. Oglethorpe negotiated a peace agreement with the Yamacraw chief Tomochichi, facilitated by the interpreter Mary Musgrove. The colony initially banned slavery and hard liquor, though the slavery prohibition was later repealed in 1751.
key_figures: James Oglethorpe (founder and de facto governor)

sources.name: James Oglethorpe, A New and Accurate Account of the Provinces of South-Carolina and Georgia (1733)
Phinizy Spalding, Oglethorpe in America (1977)
Georgia colonial charter (1732) and Oglethorpe's correspondence with the Trustees

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in colonial records, charter documents, and Oglethorpe's own writings.

events.title: British Parliament passes the Molasses Act
events.description: The British Parliament enacted the Molasses Act on 25 May 1733, imposing a duty of sixpence per gallon on molasses imported into British colonies from non-British Caribbean colonies, particularly French and Dutch sugar islands. The act was lobbied for by British West Indian sugar planters who faced competition from cheaper French molasses. The tax was widely evaded by colonial merchants and proved nearly impossible to enforce, but it established a precedent for parliamentary taxation of the colonies that later contributed to revolutionary grievances. It was succeeded by the more aggressively enforced Sugar Act of 1764.
key_figures: British West Indian planter interest (lobbying bloc)

sources.name: Molasses Act, 6 Geo. II c. 13 (Parliamentary statute)
Thomas C. Barrow, Trade and Empire: The British Customs Service in Colonial America (1967)

events.certainty_note: Statutory text survives; date and provisions are established beyond doubt.

events.title: John Kay patents the flying shuttle
events.description: In 1733, John Kay of Bury patented the flying shuttle, a weaving device that used a spring-loaded mechanism to propel the shuttle across the loom automatically. This allowed a single weaver to operate a broadloom that previously required two workers, dramatically increasing weaving productivity. The invention is considered one of the key precursors to the Industrial Revolution in textile manufacturing, as it created a demand imbalance — weavers could now consume yarn faster than spinners could produce it — which later spurred innovations in spinning technology such as the spinning jenny and water frame.

sources.name: British patent records, patent no. 542 of 1733
Richard L. Hills, Power in the Industrial Revolution (1970)

events.certainty_note: Patent record survives. Exact date of patent grant within 1733 is established.

events.title: Bach dedicates the Kyrie and Gloria to Augustus III
events.description: On 27 July 1733, Johann Sebastian Bach sent a petition to the new Elector of Saxony, Augustus III, accompanied by the manuscript parts for a Missa (Kyrie and Gloria) in B minor. Bach sought the title of court composer, which he eventually received in 1736. These movements later became the first two sections of his Mass in B minor (BWV 232), widely regarded as one of the greatest works of Western sacred music. The dedication was part of Bach's strategy to gain royal patronage and improve his standing relative to the Leipzig city council, with which he had ongoing disputes.
key_figures: Augustus III (Elector of Saxony, also disputed King of Poland)

sources.name: Bach's dedicatory letter to Augustus III, 27 July 1733 (autograph manuscript, Sächsisches Staatsarchiv Dresden)
Christoph Wolff, Johann Sebastian Bach: The Learned Musician (2000)

events.certainty_note: Bach's dedicatory letter and the original parts survive in the Saxon State Archive.

events.title: Voltaire's Letters Concerning the English Nation published
events.description: In 1733, Voltaire's Letters Concerning the English Nation (Lettres sur les Anglais) was published in English in London. The work, based on his exile in England (1726–1729), praised English constitutional monarchy, religious toleration, Newtonian science, and Lockean philosophy as implicit critiques of French absolutism and Catholic orthodoxy. The French edition (Lettres philosophiques) appeared in 1734 and was immediately condemned and publicly burned. The work is considered a foundational Enlightenment text that introduced French readers to English empiricism and liberalism.

sources.name: Voltaire, Letters Concerning the English Nation (London, 1733)
Nicholas Cronk, Voltaire: A Very Short Introduction (2017)

events.certainty_note: The English edition's 1733 date is established. Some scholarship notes copies may have circulated in late 1733 with a 1734 title page; the French edition definitively appeared in 1734.

events.title: Russian forces enter Poland to support Augustus III
events.description: In September–October 1733, Empress Anna of Russia dispatched approximately 30,000 troops under General Peter Lacy into Poland to oppose the election of Stanisław Leszczyński and install Augustus III of Saxony. Russian intervention was decisive: Leszczyński was forced to flee Warsaw to Danzig (Gdańsk), where he awaited French relief that proved inadequate. The Russian military intervention underscored the growing dominance of Russia over Polish-Lithuanian affairs, a pattern that would intensify throughout the 18th century, culminating in the partitions of Poland.
key_figures: Burkhard Christoph von Münnich (Russian field marshal)

sources.name: Russian Imperial War Ministry records and contemporary diplomatic correspondence
John L. Sutton, The King's Honor and the King's Cardinal (1980)

events.certainty_note: Military movements well documented in Russian, Polish, and Western European sources.

events.title: Spanish conquest of Naples begins
events.description: As part of the War of the Polish Succession, Spain launched a campaign to reconquer the Kingdom of Naples from Austrian Habsburg rule. The Spanish Infante Don Carlos (the future Charles III of Spain), supported by his mother Elisabeth Farnese's relentless diplomacy, led an army into southern Italy in late 1733. The campaign succeeded rapidly; by 1734 Don Carlos entered Naples in triumph and was crowned King of Naples and Sicily, ending two centuries of Habsburg rule over southern Italy and establishing an independent Bourbon kingdom there.
key_figures: Don Carlos (Infante of Spain, future Charles III)
Elisabeth Farnese (Queen of Spain, architect of the Italian policy)

sources.name: Harold Acton, The Bourbons of Naples (1956)
Contemporary Spanish and Austrian diplomatic and military correspondence

events.certainty_note: Well-documented military campaign. The exact timing of the initial advance in late 1733 versus early 1734 is sometimes conflated in sources, but the campaign's initiation in 1733 is established.

events.title: Yongzheng Emperor consolidates Qing control over Tibet and Mongolia
events.description: The Yongzheng Emperor continued his policies of consolidating Qing authority over frontier regions. In the early 1730s, the Qing administration was managing the aftermath of the 1727–1728 Tibetan civil war by strengthening the amban (imperial resident) system in Lhasa and reorganizing military garrisons. In Mongolia, Qing authorities worked to contain the Dzungar Khanate's influence. The Yongzheng reign (1722–1735) was marked by administrative reform, anti-corruption campaigns, and the consolidation of the system of ruling through Tibetan Buddhist institutions while maintaining direct military oversight.

sources.name: Qing Shilu (Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty)
Peter C. Perdue, China Marches West: The Qing Conquest of Central Eurasia (2005)

events.certainty_note: Qing frontier policies in this period are well documented, but specific actions assigned to 1733 versus adjacent years depend on interpretation of administrative records. The broader pattern is firmly established.

events.title: Tokugawa Yoshimune's Kyōhō Reforms continue in Japan
events.description: Shogun Tokugawa Yoshimune continued implementing the Kyōhō Reforms (1716–1745), a comprehensive program to stabilize the shogunate's finances and regulate rice prices. In the early 1730s, Yoshimune relaxed the ban on importing Western books (except those concerning Christianity), allowing the study of Dutch learning (rangaku) to expand. He also promoted agricultural reclamation projects and implemented a system of rice price regulation. The year 1733 saw growing urban unrest related to rice prices, foreshadowing the major Edo rice riots of 1733 (Kyōhō famine riots), in which impoverished townspeople attacked rice merchants.

sources.name: Tokugawa Jikki (official chronicle of the Tokugawa shoguns)
Harold Bolitho, 'The Tempō Crisis,' in Marius Jansen (ed.), The Cambridge History of Japan, Vol. 5 (1989); and Conrad Totman, Early Modern Japan (1993)

events.certainty_note: The Kyōhō famine riots of 1733 are documented in Japanese urban records. The reform program is extensively attested.

events.title: Dahomey consolidates power on the West African coast
events.description: Under King Agaja (r. 1718–1740), the Kingdom of Dahomey continued to consolidate its control over the coastal slave-trading ports, having conquered the kingdoms of Allada (1724) and Whydah (1727). By the early 1730s, Dahomey was managing complex relationships with European slave traders and the more powerful Oyo Empire to the northeast, to which Dahomey paid tribute. Agaja's reign saw the transformation of Dahomey from an inland kingdom to a major participant in the Atlantic slave trade, though some historians have debated whether Agaja sought to control or limit the trade.

sources.name: Robin Law, The Slave Coast of West Africa, 1550–1750 (1991)
European trading company records (English, French, and Dutch factory correspondence from Whydah)

events.certainty_note: Dahomey's general situation in this period is well attested through European trading records and later oral traditions, but precise events within 1733 are difficult to isolate.

events.title: Abraham de Moivre approximates the normal distribution
events.description: In 1733, Abraham de Moivre privately circulated a paper (later incorporated into the second edition of his Doctrine of Chances, 1738) in which he derived the normal curve as an approximation to the binomial distribution. This was the first statement of the central limit theorem in a special case and the first description of the bell-shaped curve that would later become fundamental to probability theory and statistics. De Moivre's work preceded the independent derivations by Laplace and Gauss and is considered a foundational contribution to mathematical statistics.
key_figures: Abraham de Moivre (French Huguenot mathematician, based in London)

sources.name: Abraham de Moivre, 'Approximatio ad Summam Terminorum Binomii (a+b)^n in Seriem expansi' (privately circulated pamphlet, 1733)
Stephen M. Stigler, The History of Statistics: The Measurement of Uncertainty before 1900 (1986)

events.certainty_note: The 1733 pamphlet survives and its mathematical content is well analyzed by historians of mathematics.

events.title: Nadir consolidates power in Persia under Abbas III
events.description: In 1733, Nadir (not yet Shah) continued to exercise de facto power over Persia as regent for the infant Safavid puppet Shah Abbas III, whom he had installed after deposing Tahmasp II in 1732. Nadir was engaged in campaigns to recover territories lost to the Ottoman Empire during the collapse of Safavid authority, including efforts to retake Baghdad and territories in the Caucasus. His military campaigns against the Ottomans in Mesopotamia in 1733 met with mixed results, but demonstrated the reviving power of the Persian state under his leadership.
key_figures: Nadir (regent of Persia, future Nadir Shah)
Abbas III (infant Safavid Shah, puppet ruler)

sources.name: Mirza Mahdi Khan Astarabadi, Tarikh-e Jahangoshay-e Naderi
Michael Axworthy, The Sword of Persia: Nader Shah (2006)

events.certainty_note: Nadir's campaigns in 1733 are documented in Persian court chronicles and Ottoman records.

events.title: Mughal Emperor Muhammad Shah faces Maratha expansion
events.description: The Mughal Empire under Muhammad Shah (r. 1719–1748) continued to lose effective control over its provinces as the Maratha Confederacy expanded northward and regional nawabs asserted autonomy. By the early 1730s, the Marathas under Peshwa Baji Rao I were extracting chauth (tribute) from Mughal provinces across central India and making incursions toward Delhi. The Nizam of Hyderabad, Asaf Jah I, operated as a virtually independent ruler in the Deccan. This fragmentation left the Mughal Empire increasingly vulnerable to later invasions by Nadir Shah (1739) and Ahmad Shah Durrani.

sources.name: Tarikh-i Muhammad Shahi and other Mughal court chronicles
Stewart Gordon, The Marathas 1600–1818 (1993), New Cambridge History of India

events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Maratha expansion and Mughal decline in this period is well established, though assigning specific events precisely to 1733 versus adjacent years is sometimes difficult.

events.title: Girolamo Saccheri publishes work on non-Euclidean geometry
events.description: In 1733, the Jesuit mathematician Girolamo Saccheri published Euclides ab Omni Naevo Vindicatus ('Euclid Freed of Every Flaw'), in which he attempted to prove Euclid's parallel postulate by reductio ad absurdum. In doing so, he unknowingly developed many theorems of what would later be recognized as hyperbolic and elliptic geometry. Although Saccheri rejected his own results as 'repugnant to the nature of the straight line,' his work is now considered a crucial precursor to the non-Euclidean geometries developed by Lobachevsky, Bolyai, and Riemann a century later.

sources.name: Girolamo Saccheri, Euclides ab Omni Naevo Vindicatus (Milan, 1733)
Jeremy Gray, Ideas of Space: Euclidean, Non-Euclidean and Relativistic (1989)

events.certainty_note: Publication date and content firmly established. The work was largely forgotten until rediscovered by Beltrami in the 1880s.

events.title: Corsican revolt against Genoese rule
events.description: Corsican unrest against Genoese rule continued in 1733, part of a prolonged cycle of revolts that had intensified since 1729. Corsican leaders demanded fiscal reform and greater autonomy. Genoa, unable to suppress the rebellion with its own forces, would eventually seek outside intervention. These revolts laid the groundwork for Corsica's brief period of independence under Pasquale Paoli in the 1750s–1760s and its eventual sale to France in 1768, just a year before the birth of Napoleon Bonaparte.
key_figures: Giacinto Paoli (Corsican leader, father of Pasquale Paoli)

sources.name: Genoese colonial administrative records
Dorothy Carrington, Granite Island: A Portrait of Corsica (1971)

events.certainty_note: The Corsican revolts of 1729–1769 are well documented, though specific actions in 1733 versus adjacent years can be difficult to isolate precisely.

events.title: Stephen Hales publishes Haemastaticks
events.description: The Reverend Stephen Hales published Haemastaticks in 1733, a pioneering work in which he measured blood pressure in animals for the first time and studied the mechanics of circulation. Building on his earlier Vegetable Staticks (1727), Hales used glass tubes inserted into the arteries of horses and other animals to measure the force of blood flow. The work was a landmark in experimental physiology, applying quantitative methods to biological systems, and influenced subsequent cardiovascular research for over a century.

sources.name: Stephen Hales, Statical Essays: Containing Haemastaticks (London, 1733)
D. G. C. Allan and R. E. Schofield, Stephen Hales: Scientist and Philanthropist (1980)

events.certainty_note: Publication date firmly established; the book is extant and widely referenced in the history of medicine.

disconfirming_evidence: Several points of scholarly debate surround events listed here. (1) The Molasses Act (1733-006): Some economic historians argue its significance as a precursor to the American Revolution is overstated, since it was largely unenforced and colonial merchants evaded it routinely; the real turning point came with the Sugar Act of 1764 and its enforcement mechanisms. (2) King Agaja of Dahomey (1733-014): Robin Law and other historians have debated whether Agaja's coastal conquests were motivated by a desire to control and profit from the slave trade or to limit it; earlier interpretations (notably by I.A. Akinjogbin) suggesting Agaja opposed the slave trade are now considered insufficiently supported by the evidence. (3) John Kay's flying shuttle (1733-007): While the patent date of 1733 is confirmed, some historians note that widespread adoption did not occur until the 1750s–1760s, and the narrative of the flying shuttle as the 'trigger' of the Industrial Revolution is a simplification that understates the role of prior incremental innovations in weaving and spinning. (4) Saccheri's geometry (1733-018): The characterization of Saccheri as a 'precursor to non-Euclidean geometry' is retrospective; Saccheri himself explicitly rejected the implications of his work, and his influence on Lobachevsky and Bolyai is indirect at best, since the work was largely unknown until Beltrami's rediscovery.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1733 is rich for Western Europe, with extensive diplomatic correspondence, parliamentary records, patent files, and published works surviving. The major gap lies in non-elite and non-European perspectives: the experiences of enslaved Africans, Indigenous peoples of the Americas, and common people across Asia are largely invisible in the archival record, which is dominated by state, church, and commercial documentation. For Poland-Lithuania, the 1733 succession crisis is well documented but predominantly through the lens of the intervening powers (Russia, France, Austria), with Polish-language sources receiving less attention in Anglophone scholarship. For West Africa, European trading company records provide significant but inherently biased documentation of the Dahomean kingdom. For Qing China, the Shilu and provincial records are extensive but their systematic study by Western historians is unevenly developed. Persian sources for Nadir's campaigns exist but are court-sponsored and correspondingly partial.

graph_edges.note: Augustus II's death triggered the contested election of Leszczyński
The disputed Polish election escalated into the War of the Polish Succession
The War of the Polish Succession prompted the First Family Compact between France and Spain
Russian intervention in Poland was a theatre of the War of the Polish Succession
The Spanish campaign in Naples was part of the War of the Polish Succession
The Molasses Act was a precursor to the Sugar Act of 1764 and the broader colonial taxation disputes
The flying shuttle's productivity increase contributed to the demand for mechanized spinning, spurring later inventions
Nadir's consolidation of power as regent led to his coronation as Shah in 1736
Mughal fragmentation and weakness enabled Nadir Shah's devastating invasion in 1739
Georgia became one of the thirteen colonies that declared independence in 1776
Bach's 1733 Missa dedication eventually secured him the title of Saxon court composer in 1736
